name: short_traveling_male_age_55_med_management_47bdc3

transcript.speaker: Clinician
transcript.text: Please let him know I’ve ordered the 0.25-mg semaglutide pen. He’ll inject once on Tuesday evenings right after dialysis, not before, so the fluid shift doesn’t amplify nausea. Metformin stays discontinued. Remind him carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily, sevelamer 800 mg with each meal, and the rosuvastatin 20 mg at bedtime stay unchanged. If he feels queasy, he can halve the next meal, sip ginger tea, and call if vomiting lasts more than one day.

transcript.speaker: Clinician
transcript.text: Also, ask whether his travel plans will interrupt dialysis; if he’s flying, airports can, uh, delay refrigerated meds. The pen is stable at room temperature for 28 days, but he should still keep it in the carry-on, not checked luggage. Weight may drop in the first two weeks—under ten pounds is fine; more than that, or dizziness, and I’ll need a quick tele-visit. Have him keep a daily glucose log.

limited_chart.demographicStr: the patient is a man, born on January 1, 1969 (age 55)

currentConditions.label: End stage renal disease on dialysis

currentGoals.label: Improve blood sugar control with weekly semaglutide
Monitor nausea and weight changes around dialysis sessions

currentMedications.label: Semaglutide 2 mg/1.6 mL subcutaneous solution (weekly injection)

profile: During evening dialysis, a 55-year-old man with limited English reviews his medications through an interpreter. Metformin was stopped, and today the nephrologist begins weekly semaglutide injections to curb persistent high sugar. He also takes carvedilol, sevelamer, and a nightly statin; no drug allergies, but nausea and weight change will be monitored around dialysis timings.
